article_title: Hackers Target TP-Link Routers With Mirai Malware in CVE-2023-33538 Exploitation Attempts
core_thesis: Attackers are actively exploiting CVE-2023-33538, a command injection vulnerability in end-of-life TP-Link routers (TL-WR940N, TL-WR740N, TL-WR841N), to deploy Condi IoT botnet malware (a Mirai variant). The vulnerability lies in the web management interface's improper validation of the ssid parameter in HTTP GET requests to /userRpm/WlanNetworkRpm. Despite technical errors in observed exploits (targeting wrong parameter and missing wget), the vulnerability is real and poses a significant risk to unpatched devices, which will never receive official fixes.
detailed_summary: The article reports that hackers are actively exploiting CVE-2023-33538, a command injection vulnerability in several end-of-life TP-Link router models (TL-WR940N v2/v4, TL-WR740N v1/v2, TL-WR841N v8/v10). The flaw exists in the web management interface, where the ssid parameter in an HTTP GET request to /userRpm/WlanNetworkRpm is not sanitized, allowing attackers to inject arbitrary commands. The attacks aim to install a Mirai-based botnet malware called Condi, which is delivered as an ELF binary (arm7) from IP 51.38.137[.]113. Unit 42 (Palo Alto Networks) detected large-scale exploitation attempts after CISA added the CVE to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog in June 2025. The malware connects to C2 domain cnc.vietdediserver[.]shop, performs self-updates for multiple architectures, and runs an HTTP server on a random port to spread to other devices. Notably, observed exploits contained errors: they targeted the ssid parameter instead of the correct ssid1 parameter, and used wget which is not available in the router's BusyBox environment. However, the vulnerability is confirmed exploitable with correct parameters. TP-Link has marked these devices as end-of-life and will not release patches; users are advised to replace them and change default credentials.

key_data_points: CVE-2023-33538 is a command injection vulnerability in the web management interface of TP-Link TL-WR940N (v2/v4), TL-WR740N (v1/v2), and TL-WR841N (v8/v10) routers, caused by improper validation of the ssid parameter in HTTP GET requests to /userRpm/WlanNetworkRpm. (🟢 Red Teaming: This is a classic command injection vector that can be weaponized for initial access to IoT devices; understanding the exact endpoint and parameter is crucial for exploitation.)

The observed exploit attempts targeted the ssid parameter instead of the correct ssid1 parameter, and used wget which is not present in the router's BusyBox environment, indicating technical errors by the attackers. However, the vulnerability is real and exploitable with correct parameters. (🟢 Red Teaming: Even flawed attempts highlight the attack surface; a skilled attacker can correct these errors and achieve reliable exploitation.)

The downloaded arm7 binary is a Condi IoT botnet variant (Mirai-based) that connects to C2 domain cnc.vietdediserver[.]shop, performs self-updates for eight CPU architectures (arm6, mips, sh4, x86_64, etc.) from hard-coded IP 51.38.137[.]113:80, and runs an HTTP server on a random port (1024-65535) to serve malware to other devices. (🟢 Red Teaming: The self-update and peer-to-peer spreading mechanisms are key behaviors to emulate in red team operations; the hard-coded IP and C2 domain can be used for detection or sinkholing.)

TP-Link confirmed the affected routers are end-of-life and will not receive patches; the only mitigation is to replace them and change default admin credentials. (🟢 Homelab/DevOps: This underscores the importance of asset lifecycle management and replacing unsupported devices; for homelabs, it's a reminder to audit and retire outdated hardware.)
methodology_steps: 1. Identify vulnerable TP-Link router models (TL-WR940N v2/v4, TL-WR740N v1/v2, TL-WR841N v8/v10) by checking firmware version or model number.

2. Exploit CVE-2023-33538 by sending a crafted HTTP GET request to http://<router-ip>/userRpm/WlanNetworkRpm with a malicious ssid1 parameter containing command injection payloads (e.g., using backticks or $() to execute commands).

3. The injected command should download an ELF binary (e.g., arm7) from an attacker-controlled server using available tools like tftp or curl (since wget may be absent), assign execute permissions (chmod +x), and run it.
4. The malware binary will connect to a C2 server, await commands, and optionally start an HTTP server on a random port to spread to other vulnerable devices.
5. For defense: monitor outbound connections to known malicious IPs/domains (51.38.137[.]113, cnc.vietdediserver[.]shop), block access to the vulnerable endpoint, and replace end-of-life routers.
conclusions: CVE-2023-33538 is a serious command injection vulnerability in end-of-life TP-Link routers that is being actively exploited to deploy Mirai-based botnet malware. Despite errors in current attacks, the vulnerability is fully exploitable and poses a persistent threat to unpatched devices. (🟢 Red Teaming: This is a prime candidate for inclusion in red team toolkits targeting IoT devices; the exploitation technique is straightforward and can be automated.)

The Condi botnet's self-update and peer-to-peer propagation mechanisms make it resilient and capable of rapid spread. Understanding these behaviors aids in both offensive emulation and defensive detection. (🟢 Red Teaming: Emulating the self-update routine can help test network segmentation and egress filtering.)

The only effective mitigation is hardware replacement, as no patches will be issued. This highlights the importance of vendor lifecycle management and the risks of using end-of-life devices in any network. (🟢 Homelab/DevOps: Homelab users should audit their network for unsupported devices and plan upgrades to maintain security.)
personal_relevance_summary: This article is directly relevant to your Red Teaming and cybersecurity interests, as it details a real-world command injection vulnerability (CVE-2023-33538) in TP-Link routers that is actively exploited to deploy Mirai-based botnet malware. You can leverage this knowledge to build or enhance your own exploitation modules for IoT devices, understand common pitfalls (like parameter mismatches and missing utilities), and practice crafting reliable payloads. For your homelab, it serves as a cautionary tale to retire end-of-life equipment and implement proper network segmentation. As a student, you can study the malware's self-update and peer-to-peer propagation mechanisms to deepen your understanding of botnet operations and defensive strategies.
extra_advice: Consider setting up a lab with an affected TP-Link router (or emulated firmware) to practice exploiting CVE-2023-33538 and deploying a custom payload. This hands-on experience will sharpen your command injection skills and give you insight into IoT botnet mechanics. Additionally, you could write a detection rule (e.g., for Suricata or Zeek) that flags HTTP GET requests to /userRpm/WlanNetworkRpm with suspicious ssid1 parameters, which would be a valuable addition to your red team toolkit.
